0.question: Give a definition for the term "artificial neural network" and mention, how it resembles the human brain!

concepts.name: Artificial Neural Network
concepts.description: A massively parallel distributed processor made of simple processing units.

concepts.name: Processing Unit
concepts.description: Simple components that make up an artificial neural network.

concepts.name: Experiential Knowledge
concepts.description: Knowledge acquired by a neural network from its environment.

concepts.name: Human Brain
concepts.description: Biological system that artificial neural networks are modeled after.

concepts.name: Learning Process
concepts.description: Mechanism by which neural networks acquire knowledge from their environment.

concepts.name: Synaptic Weights
concepts.description: Interneuron connection strengths used to store acquired knowledge in neural networks.

relationships.description: An ANN is composed of simple processing units.
ANNs have a natural propensity for storing experiential knowledge.
ANNs resemble the human brain in certain aspects.
Knowledge is acquired through a learning process.
The learning process results in acquired knowledge.
Synaptic weights are used to store acquired knowledge.

1.question: Define the mathematical model of a neuron, use the appropriate technical terms!

concepts.name: Mathematical Model of a Neuron
concepts.description: A model consisting of synapses, an adder function, and an activation function.

concepts.name: Synapses
concepts.description: Connecting links in a neuron model, each characterized by a weight.

concepts.name: Weight
concepts.description: A characteristic value associated with each synaptic link.

concepts.name: Adder Function
concepts.description: A linear combiner that computes the weighted sum of inputs plus bias.

concepts.name: Weighted Sum
concepts.description: The local field computed by the adder function.

concepts.name: Bias
concepts.description: An additional term added to the weighted sum of inputs.

concepts.name: Activation Function
concepts.description: A squashing function that limits the amplitude of a neuron's output.

concepts.name: Neuron Output
concepts.description: The final output of the neuron after the activation function.

relationships.description: The model includes a set of synapses.
Each link (synapse) is characterized by a weight.
The model includes an adder function.
The adder function computes the weighted sum.
The weighted sum includes some bias.
The model includes an activation function.
The activation function limits the amplitude of the neuron's output.

2.question: Assume you want to binary classify linear separable data point using a perceptron. Write down the learning algorithm in pseudo code!

concepts.name: Perceptron
concepts.description: A linear classifier used for binary classification of linearly separable data.

concepts.name: Binary Classification
concepts.description: The task of classifying data into two distinct categories.

concepts.name: Linearly Separable Data
concepts.description: Data points that can be perfectly separated by a linear decision boundary.

concepts.name: Learning Algorithm
concepts.description: A set of rules for a perceptron to learn from data.

concepts.name: Data Labeling
concepts.description: Assigning positive and negative labels to data points.

concepts.name: Weight Initialization
concepts.description: Randomly setting the initial values of the perceptron's weights.

concepts.name: Update Rule
concepts.description: The rule for adjusting weights based on misclassifications (e.g., Dw = eta*x(n)).

concepts.name: Convergence
concepts.description: The state where weights no longer change significantly, indicating the algorithm has learned.

relationships.description: A perceptron is used for binary classification.
The perceptron algorithm is applied to linearly separable data.
A perceptron employs a specific learning algorithm.
Data must be labeled before applying the algorithm.
Weights must be initialized randomly.
The algorithm includes an update rule for weights.
The algorithm repeats until convergence.
The algorithm will converge if the data is linearly separable.

3.question: Explain classification and regression; what is the difference?

concepts.name: Classification
concepts.description: A machine learning task of mapping data to discrete labels.

concepts.name: Regression
concepts.description: A machine learning task of mapping data to a continuous function or real values.

concepts.name: Discrete Labels
concepts.description: Distinct, separate categories used in classification.

concepts.name: Continuous Values
concepts.description: Real numbers or a continuous function output used in regression.

concepts.name: Classification Error
concepts.description: The number of misclassifications in a classification task.

concepts.name: Regression Error
concepts.description: The summed distance between true and predicted values in a regression task.

relationships.description: Classification maps data to discrete labels.
Regression maps data to continuous values.
Classification and regression are distinct tasks.
Error in classification is measured by misclassifications.
Error in regression is measured by summed distance.

4.question: Write down the SOM learning in pseudo code.

concepts.name: SOM Learning
concepts.description: The learning algorithm for Self-Organizing Maps.

concepts.name: Weight Topology
concepts.description: The arrangement of weights in the SOM lattice.

concepts.name: Weight Initialization
concepts.description: Randomly setting initial, distinct weights for neurons.

concepts.name: Input Sampling
concepts.description: Drawing an input vector from the input space.

concepts.name: Similarity Matching
concepts.description: Finding the Best Matching Unit (BMU) or winning neuron in the lattice.

concepts.name: Winning Neuron
concepts.description: The neuron in the topological lattice that best matches the input.

concepts.name: Weight Update
concepts.description: Adjusting the weights of the winning neuron and its neighbors.

concepts.name: Neighborhood Function
concepts.description: Determines the extent and influence of neighbors during weight updates.

concepts.name: Learning Rate Decay
concepts.description: Gradually reducing the learning rate over time.

concepts.name: Convergence
concepts.description: The state where the SOM has learned the input distribution and ordering is complete.

relationships.description: Weights must be arranged in a topology.
Weights must be initialized randomly.
The algorithm samples input from the input space.
A key step is similarity matching to find the winning neuron.
Similarity matching identifies the winning neuron.
The algorithm updates weights of the winning neuron and neighbors.
The neighborhood function determines which neighbors are updated.
The learning rate and neighborhood radius are reduced.
The algorithm stops when ordering and convergence are complete.

5.question: Give the basic idea of an SVM using the correct terminology!

concepts.name: Support Vector Machine (SVM)
concepts.description: A linear learnable machine that uses a decision boundary with maximum margin for classification.

concepts.name: Linear Learnable Machine
concepts.description: A type of machine learning model that can classify data using a linear function.

concepts.name: Decision Boundary
concepts.description: A hyperplane that separates data points into different classes.

concepts.name: Maximum Margin
concepts.description: The largest possible distance between the decision boundary and the nearest data points of any class.

concepts.name: Support Vectors
concepts.description: Data points that are closest to the decision boundary and determine the margin.

concepts.name: Kernels
concepts.description: Functions used to transform non-linearly separable data into a higher dimension where it becomes linearly separable.

concepts.name: Quadratic Programming
concepts.description: An optimization technique used to solve the SVM algorithm.

concepts.name: Non-Linearly Separable Data
concepts.description: Data that cannot be separated by a single linear decision boundary in its original feature space.

relationships.description: SVMs are a type of linear learnable machine.
SVMs use a decision boundary for classification.
The decision boundary in SVMs aims for maximum margin.
The margin is determined by support vectors.
Kernels are used in SVMs for non-linearly separable data.
Kernels help separate non-linearly separable data.
The SVM algorithm is solved using Quadratic Programming.

6.question: What role does the method of steepest decent have when learning a network?

concepts.name: Steepest Descent
concepts.description: An optimization method used to update weights in a neural network during learning.

concepts.name: Neural Network Learning
concepts.description: The process of adjusting weights in a neural network to minimize a cost function.

concepts.name: Weight Update
concepts.description: The modification of synaptic weights in a neural network.

concepts.name: Cost Function
concepts.description: A function that quantifies the error of a neural network's predictions.

concepts.name: Gradient Vector
concepts.description: A vector pointing in the direction of the steepest increase of a function.

concepts.name: Local Minima
concepts.description: Points in the cost function landscape where the gradient is zero, but it's not the global minimum.

concepts.name: Instability
concepts.description: A potential issue where the learning process diverges instead of converging.

relationships.description: Steepest descent is used to update weights.
Steepest descent is a method for NN learning.
It helps navigate the cost function to find a minimum.
Weights are updated opposite to the gradient vector.
Steepest descent can suffer from local minima.
The method may become unstable under certain conditions.

7.question: Define: a hypothesis $h \in H$ shatters a dataset $A \subseteq X \Leftrightarrow \ldots$

concepts.name: Hypothesis (h)
concepts.description: A function from a hypothesis space H that attempts to classify data.

concepts.name: Hypothesis Space (H)
concepts.description: The set of all possible hypotheses.

concepts.name: Dataset (A)
concepts.description: A subset of the input space X, consisting of N data points.

concepts.name: Input Space (X)
concepts.description: The domain from which data points are drawn.

concepts.name: Shattering a Dataset
concepts.description: The ability of a hypothesis to perfectly classify all possible binary labelings of a dataset.

concepts.name: Binary Labeling
concepts.description: Assigning positive or negative labels to data points, resulting in 2^N possibilities for N points.

concepts.name: No Error Classification
concepts.description: Perfectly splitting positive data from negative data without any misclassifications.

relationships.description: A hypothesis is an element of the hypothesis space.
A dataset is a subset of the input space.
A hypothesis shatters a dataset.
Shattering applies to a dataset.
Shattering implies classifying all possible binary labelings.
Shattering means splitting data with no error.

8.question: Write down and explain the Widrow-Hoff learning rule!

concepts.name: Widrow-Hoff Learning Rule
concepts.description: A learning rule where synaptic weight adjustment is proportional to the product of error and input signals.

concepts.name: Synaptic Weight Adjustment
concepts.description: The change made to a synaptic weight during learning.

concepts.name: Error Signal
concepts.description: The difference between the desired output and the actual output of a neuron.

concepts.name: Input Signal
concepts.description: The signal received at a specific synapse.

concepts.name: Steepest Descent Method
concepts.description: An optimization method from which the Widrow-Hoff rule is derived.

relationships.description: The rule defines how synaptic weights are adjusted.
Adjustment is proportional to the error signal.
Adjustment is proportional to the input signal.
The rule is derived from the steepest descent method.

9.question: Explain back propagation, use the correct technical terms!

concepts.name: Backpropagation
concepts.description: An algorithm that recursively lowers the error of a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) by propagating error backwards.

concepts.name: Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)
concepts.description: A type of feedforward artificial neural network with multiple layers of neurons.

concepts.name: Error Reduction
concepts.description: The process of minimizing the difference between predicted and actual outputs.

concepts.name: Weight Update
concepts.description: Adjusting the synaptic weights of neurons in the network.

concepts.name: Local Gradient
concepts.description: The partial derivative of the error with respect to a specific weight at each layer.

concepts.name: Partial Derivatives
concepts.description: Mathematical tools used to compute the local gradient.

concepts.name: Chain Rule
concepts.description: A calculus rule used to compute derivatives of composite functions, essential for backpropagation.

relationships.description: Backpropagation is used to train MLPs.
Backpropagation lowers the error level by level.
It updates weights by propagating error backwards.
Weight updates are determined by the local gradient.
Local gradients are computed using partial derivatives.
Local gradients are computed using the chain rule.

10.question: When learning using steepest descent, explain the role of the learning rate? What is a danger?

concepts.name: Steepest Descent Learning
concepts.description: An optimization algorithm used in machine learning to find the minimum of a function.

concepts.name: Learning Rate
concepts.description: A hyperparameter that controls the step size during weight updates in steepest descent.

concepts.name: Convergence Speed
concepts.description: How quickly the learning algorithm reaches a stable solution or minimum.

concepts.name: Overdamped Convergence
concepts.description: Slow convergence due to a very low learning rate.

concepts.name: Underdamped Convergence
concepts.description: Zigzagging convergence due to a high learning rate.

concepts.name: Instability
concepts.description: A state where learning diverges, occurring when the learning rate exceeds a critical value.

relationships.description: The learning rate controls the speed of convergence.
A low learning rate leads to overdamped convergence.
A high learning rate leads to underdamped convergence.
Exceeding a critical learning rate causes instability.
Steepest descent learning utilizes a learning rate.

11.question: How does a Reduced Boltzman Machine work (main idea)?

concepts.name: Reduced Boltzmann Machine (RBM)
concepts.description: A bi-parted graph that works by flipping states of binary neurons based on activation probability.

concepts.name: Bi-Parted Graph
concepts.description: A graph structure with two distinct sets of nodes (layers) where connections only exist between sets.

concepts.name: Binary Neurons
concepts.description: Neurons that can exist in one of two states (e.g., 0 or 1).

concepts.name: State Flipping
concepts.description: The process of changing the state of a binary neuron based on a probability.

concepts.name: Activation Probability
concepts.description: The probability that a neuron will be in an 'on' state, determined by its activation.

concepts.name: Visible Layer
concepts.description: The layer of neurons in an RBM that receives input.

concepts.name: Hidden Layer
concepts.description: The layer of neurons in an RBM that learns features from the input.

concepts.name: Clamped State
concepts.description: A state where visible neurons are connected to the input.

concepts.name: Free Running State
concepts.description: A state where both visible and hidden layers run freely without external input.

relationships.description: An RBM is a bi-parted graph.
RBMs consist of binary neurons.
RBMs work by flipping neuron states.
State flipping is based on activation probability.
RBMs have a visible layer.
RBMs have a hidden layer.
Visible layer is connected to input in clamped state.
Both layers run free in free-running state.
Both layers run free in free-running state.

12.question: Define: Echo State Network (ESN), how are they different to FF NNs?

concepts.name: Echo State Network (ESN)
concepts.description: A type of Recurrent Neural Network with at least one cyclic (feedback) connection.

concepts.name: Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)
concepts.description: A class of neural networks where connections between nodes form a directed graph along a temporal sequence.

concepts.name: Cyclic Connection
concepts.description: A feedback loop in a neural network, allowing information to persist.

concepts.name: Feedforward Neural Network (FF NN)
concepts.description: A type of neural network where connections between nodes do not form a cycle.

concepts.name: Output Layer Weights
concepts.description: The weights connecting the hidden layer to the output layer in an ESN, which are the only ones updated during learning.

concepts.name: Dynamic Systems Approximation
concepts.description: The ability to model systems whose state evolves over time.

relationships.description: ESN is a type of RNN.
ESNs have at least one cyclic connection.
Only output layer weights are updated in ESN learning.
ESNs are different from FF NNs.
FF NNs do not have feedback connections.
ESNs can approximate dynamic systems.
FF NNs cannot approximate dynamic systems.

13.question: Describe: the structure on an CNN.

concepts.name: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)
concepts.description: A type of neural network consisting of multiple specialized layers for processing structured data like images.

concepts.name: Convolutional Layer
concepts.description: A layer containing kernels that convolve over the input image to extract features.

concepts.name: Kernel
concepts.description: A small matrix that slides over the input image, performing convolution.

concepts.name: Input Image
concepts.description: The raw image data fed into the CNN.

concepts.name: Activation Layer
concepts.description: A layer that applies a non-linear activation function (e.g., ReLU) to the output of the convolutional layer.

concepts.name: ReLU Activation
concepts.description: Rectified Linear Unit, a common non-linear activation function.

concepts.name: Pooling Layer
concepts.description: A layer that reduces the spatial dimensions of the feature maps (e.g., max or average pooling).

concepts.name: Max Pooling
concepts.description: A type of pooling that takes the maximum value from each patch of the feature map.

concepts.name: Average Pooling
concepts.description: A type of pooling that takes the average value from each patch of the feature map.

concepts.name: Fully Connected Layer
concepts.description: A traditional neural network layer where every neuron is connected to every neuron in the previous layer.

concepts.name: Softmax Layer
concepts.description: An output layer that converts raw scores into probabilities for multi-class classification.

relationships.description: A CNN consists of convolutional layers.
Convolutional layers have kernels.
Kernels convolve over the input image.
A CNN includes activation layers.
ReLU is a common activation used.
A CNN includes pooling layers.
Max pooling is a type of pooling.
Average pooling is a type of pooling.
A CNN typically has one or more fully connected layers.
A CNN often ends with a softmax layer.

14.question: What are three items to be learned for an RBF network? Difference to other NNs, Pros/cons?

concepts.name: RBF Network
concepts.description: A type of neural network that uses radial basis functions as activation functions in its hidden layer.

concepts.name: Centers
concepts.description: The central points of the radial basis functions, which are learned during training.

concepts.name: Weights
concepts.description: The connection strengths between the hidden layer and the output layer, which are learned.

concepts.name: Biases
concepts.description: Additional parameters that can be learned, affecting the output of neurons.

concepts.name: Single Hidden Layer
concepts.description: A characteristic of RBF networks, having only one layer of hidden neurons.

concepts.name: Linear Output Layer
concepts.description: The output layer of an RBF network, which typically performs a linear combination.

concepts.name: Other Neural Networks
concepts.description: General term for neural networks that are not RBFNs, often having multiple hidden layers and non-linear outputs.

concepts.name: Universal Approximator
concepts.description: The property of RBFNs to approximate any continuous function to an arbitrary degree of accuracy.

concepts.name: Non-Unique Bias
concepts.description: A disadvantage of RBFNs where the bias parameter might not have a single optimal value.

relationships.description: RBF networks learn centers.
RBF networks learn weights.
RBF networks learn biases.
RBFNs have a single hidden layer.
RBFNs have a linear output layer.
RBFNs differ from other NNs in structure.
RBFNs are universal approximators (pro).
The bias in RBFNs is not unique (con).

15.question: Describe how learning based on k-nearest neighbors works, use pseudo code!

concepts.name: K-Nearest Neighbors Learning (KNN)
concepts.description: A non-parametric, instance-based learning algorithm used for classification and regression.

concepts.name: New Data Point
concepts.description: An unclassified data point that needs to be assigned a class.

concepts.name: Training Data
concepts.description: Pre-classified or colored data points used as a reference.

concepts.name: K Nearest Data Points
concepts.description: The 'k' data points in the training set that are closest to the new data point.

concepts.name: Distance Metric
concepts.description: A function used to measure the similarity or dissimilarity between data points (implied by 'construct a sphere').

concepts.name: Class Vote
concepts.description: The count of each class among the k nearest neighbors.

concepts.name: Class Assignment
concepts.description: Assigning the new data point to the class with the maximum vote.

concepts.name: Unsupervised Manner
concepts.description: A characteristic of the KNN process as described, implying it doesn't explicitly learn a model but relies on data proximity.

relationships.description: KNN is used to classify new data points.
New data is added to the existing training data.
The algorithm finds the k nearest data points to the new one.
The 'sphere' implies a distance metric is used to find neighbors.
The k nearest points contribute to a class vote.
The class with the maximum vote determines the assignment.
The process is described as working in an unsupervised manner.

concepts.name: Bias-Variance Dilemma
concepts.description: A fundamental problem in supervised learning concerning the trade-off between model bias and variance.

concepts.name: Supervised Learning
concepts.description: A type of machine learning where a model learns from labeled training data.

concepts.name: Model Bias
concepts.description: The error introduced by approximating a real-world problem, which may be complex, by a simplified model.

concepts.name: Model Variance
concepts.description: The amount that the estimate of the target function will change if different training data was used.

concepts.name: Training Data Fit
concepts.description: How well a model performs on the data it was trained on.

concepts.name: Generalizability
concepts.description: The ability of a model to perform well on unseen data.

concepts.name: High Bias
concepts.description: A state where the model is too simple and underfits the data.

concepts.name: High Variance
concepts.description: A state where the model is too complex and overfits the data.

relationships.description: The dilemma is a principle problem in supervised learning.
The dilemma arises due to model bias.
The dilemma arises due to model variance.
High bias means the model fits training data perfectly (implies overfitting).
When bias is high, the model suffers from high variance.
Bias and variance are often in opposition.
The dilemma makes generalizability difficult to achieve.
